title: Chapter 3 — Prompting Claude Like an Instructional Designer
CHAPTER 3 —
PROMPTING CLAUDE LIKE AN INSTRUCTIONAL DESIGNER
segments.body_html:
Marta teaches ninth-grade biology in a mid-sized public school. She has tried Claude a handful of times, mostly by typin

Chapter 3 — Prompting Claude Like an Ins — Marta's problem is not that Claude is bad. Her problem is that her prompts describe a topic rather than a teaching situa

beats.text: Marta teaches ninth-grade biology in a mid-sized public school. She has tried Claude a handful of times, mostly by typing something like: "Give me a lesson on cells." The results were fine , clean paragraphs, reasonable vocabulary , but they fit no one in particular. They didn't match her unit sequence, didn't address the misconception she knew her students carried (that cells are static containers rather than dynamic systems), and didn't line up with what she actually needed students to be able to do on Friday's lab assessment.

title: CORE CONCEPT —
PROMPTING AS INSTRUCTIONAL SPECIFICATION
segments.body_html: Robert Gagne→M. David Merrill→Grant Wiggins and Jay McTighe

beats.text: A prompt is not a magic phrase. It is a specification. When a software engineer writes a specification, she describes the user, the inputs, the expected behavior, the constraints, and the acceptance criteria. When an instructional designer writes a design brief, she describes the learner, the prior knowledge, the misconception, the target outcome, the activity constraints, the evidence of learning, and the review criteria. Good prompting for education is instructional specification made explicit (Anthropic prompt engineering documentation ).

segments.label: 03 / 11 · CORE CONCEPT — PROMPTING AS IN

title: THE EIGHT-FIELD
INSTRUCTIONAL BRIEF.
segments.body_html:
The following eight fields make up a reusable instructional brief. Each field prevents a specific failure.

The Eight-Field Instructional Brief — Who are these students? Grade level, course, rough context, language situation, any relevant access needs.

beats.text: The following eight fields make up a reusable instructional brief. Each field prevents a specific failure. Who are these students? Grade level, course, rough context, language situation, any relevant access needs.

segments.label: 04 / 11 · THE EIGHT-FIELD INSTRUCTIONAL

title: A WORKED
TEACHING WALKTHROUGH.
segments.body_html:
·Is the learner accurately described in the output?
·Is the prior knowledge assumption correct?
·Does the activity actually surface and address the misconception?
·Does the activity lead toward the stated outcome?
·Are the constraints respected?

beats.text: You teach a college introductory economics course. You want to build a thirty-minute discussion activity on opportunity cost. Students have read a textbook chapter. Your experience tells you that most of them understand opportunity cost as "what you give up" but apply it only to money , not to time, attention, or non-market goods. The unit assessment asks students to analyze a real decision using opportunity cost reasoning, including identification of the next-best alternative. Copy the template below and fill in each field before submitting to Claude.

segments.label: 05 / 11 · A WORKED TEACHING WALKTHROUGH

BRIEF PATTERNS.

segments.section: THE HUMAN GATE — WHAT STAYS WI
title: THE HUMAN GATE —
WHAT STAYS WITH YOU
segments.body_html:
·Outcome validity. You decide what learning matters for your students in your course context. Claude can suggest; it cann
·Misconception accuracy. You know what your students actually believe. The misconception field is only as good as your pe
·Classroom fit. Claude does not know that your third-period class moves faster than your first, that one student has a 50
·Example authenticity. Claude generates plausible examples. Plausible is not the same as true, representative, or appropr
·Tone and relationship. Claude does not know your students. Feedback that is technically accurate can still be damaging i

beats.text: Instructional specification is not a hand-off. You are creating conditions for a useful draft, not delegating instructional decisions. The following judgments are yours, not Claude's:

segments.label: 07 / 11 · THE HUMAN GATE — WHAT STAYS WI

title: COMMON MISTAKES
segments.body_html: Mistake 1: Describing a topic instead of a situation.→Mistake 2: Omitting the misconception.→Mistake 3: Accepting the first output.→Mistake 4: Forgetting to state what Claude must not do.→Mistake 5: Writing the brief once and reusing it unchanged.

beats.text: Mistake 1: Describing a topic instead of a situation. "Give me a lesson on the American Revolution" describes content. "Give me a discussion activity for tenth graders who understand the political causes but cannot yet explain why the revolution succeeded in America but not in France" describes a teaching situation. Mistake 2: Omitting the misconception. Lessons that do not address what students actually believe leave the misconception intact. Instruction that feels complete produces learning that doesn't transfer (Ambrose et al., How Learning Works).

segments.label: 08 / 11 · COMMON MISTAKES

title: BRIDGE TO
CHAPTER 4.
segments.body_html: A well-written brief produces a useful draft. Chapter 4 takes that draft and asks the harder question: does the lesson p

beats.text: A well-written brief produces a useful draft. Chapter 4 takes that draft and asks the harder question: does the lesson plan actually protect student thinking? Activity design is where briefs meet the classroom, and where the temptation to accept a polished plan , rather than audit it against student cognitive labor , is strongest.
